role_hints.role: Environmental scientists, geologists and engineers (the actual professional core)
role_hints.oews_basis: role_mix is the 4-digit 5416 proxy and badly misrepresents this code — 19-2041 Environmental Scientists, 19-2042 Geoscientists and 17-2081 Environmental Engineers do not appear in the listed top-10 but dominate real 541620 firms; est. ~30-40% of wage bill, hypothesis from industry knowledge
role_hints.note: Phase I ESA report writing, records review, regulatory database searches, permit application drafting and monitoring-report production are heavily AI-exposed; site reconnaissance, sampling oversight, agency negotiation and professional sign-off (PG/PE where required) are not. Plausible within-role fraction 0.3-0.5.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in the listed top-10 (proxy artifact); environmental science technicians (19-4042) and field staff, est. ~10-15%
role_hints.note: Physical presence — drilling oversight, sampling, monitoring well work, site walks. Sensor/drone-augmented but not replaced.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 13.5% (proxy) + tail managers
role_hints.note: Client and regulator relationships persist; internal reporting automates. Substantial owner-operator comp in the 5,655 firms with <5 employees.
Deliverable tracking, subcontractor coordination and budget paperwork automate; agency and client coordination persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in listed top-10; GIS analysts and data staff in the tail, est. ~4-6%
role_hints.note: Data reduction, mapping production and compliance-report assembly are directly automatable.
Invoicing (T&M billing admin is heavy here), proposal production, document control.

role_hints.role: Consulting/analyst overlay from the proxy mix
role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1111 Management Analysts 15.5% in the 4-digit proxy — mostly an artifact of sibling 5416 codes; treat only a small slice as real for 541620
role_hints.note: ESG/compliance advisory staff at larger firms; report and disclosure drafting automates.

source_hints.source: Environment Analyst (global environmental consulting market intelligence)
source_hints.why: Dedicated analyst coverage of the environmental consulting sector — market sizing, digitization/AI commentary and M&A tracking.
Long-running market data on the US environmental consulting & engineering industry — segment mix and growth for the terminal-value question.

source_hints.source: Zweig Group ownership transition and salary surveys (annual)
source_hints.why: Covers A/E/environmental firms — principal age and internal-transition data for owners_60plus_pct.

source_hints.source: Morrissey Goodale AE M&A data and commentary
source_hints.why: Tracks environmental consulting deals within the A/E consolidation wave — active_consolidators and deal counts.

source_hints.source: Environment Analyst M&A coverage / Equiteq ESG consulting M&A reports
source_hints.why: Environmental/ESG-specific deal multiples (Equiteq's 2025 ESG report cites private EV/EBITDA ~12x at the platform tier — small-firm entry multiples are far lower; verify).

source_hints.source: ASTM E1527-21 / EPA All Appropriate Inquiries rule (40 CFR 312)
source_hints.why: Primary documents for the 'environmental professional' requirement behind Phase I ESAs — the mechanism deciding whether AI can bypass the operator.

capture_questions: Billing is mostly T&M against rate schedules, with fixed-fee commodity work (Phase I ESAs at roughly $2-5k) and some cost-plus public contracts — when AI cuts report-production hours, does T&M rescoping and fixed-fee price competition pass the savings to clients, or do liability-bearing sign-offs let firms hold price? Estimate the revenue split across these three mechanisms.

Who owns the client relationship — firm-level MSAs with lenders, developers and industrial clients, or individual principals? Lender-driven ESA work is often program-based (bank vendor lists): institutional and sticky, or price-shopped?

Can clients bypass the firm with AI — e.g., AI-drafted Phase I reports signed by a single credentialed 'environmental professional' (the plan-stamp pattern), given AAI requires a qualified EP but not a large firm?

Does regulatory-driven demand (permits, compliance monitoring, remediation oversight) behave as a non-discretionary annuity that survives fee deflation, and what share of revenue is regulatory-mandated vs discretionary ESG advisory?
terminal_value_question: Demand here is substantially statutory: CERCLA liability defenses require AAI/Phase I ESAs by a qualified environmental professional, permits and consent decrees require monitoring and reporting, and sampling is a physical act. The 5-10 year question: does AI collapse the report-writing majority of billable hours into a small stamp-liability-and-fieldwork business — and does the 2025-2026 federal deregulatory push (NEPA and enforcement rollbacks; verify current status) shrink the statutory demand base itself? Separate AI cutting the operator's costs from regulation-driven demand disappearing.
special_notes: Role-mix caveat is severe: the record's role_mix is the 4-digit 5416 proxy dominated by management analysts — real 541620 firms are staffed by environmental scientists, geologists, engineers and field techs. Rebuild the V breakdown on the role structure above and say so.

Pricing-model deflation candidates: T&M rate schedules invite rescoping; Phase I ESA work is a fixed-fee commodity where competitive bidding passes AI savings to clients quickly; federal/state cost-plus work (EPA, DoD, DOT environmental) carries audited-overhead clawback — weight pi_dist by this mix.
EBITDA normalization: small-firm profit is SDE with owner comp inside; also markups on subcontracted drilling/lab work inflate revenue without margin — use net revenue where possible.

Consolidation is active and well documented (Montrose, Apex Companies, RSK, ERM and PE-backed platforms) — usable for B (consolidator counts) and M (multiples) only, never for V or A (rule 2); verify current pace rather than assuming 2021-2023 tempo.

Boundary confusion: remediation contractors are 562910, engineering firms doing environmental work are 541330, testing labs are 541380, ESG/sustainability strategy shops straddle 541620/541611 — 'environmental industry' surveys (EBJ) span all of these; check segment definitions before quoting.

Cyclicality/policy sensitivity: transaction-driven ESA volume tracks CRE deal flow (depressed 2023-2025), and enforcement-driven work tracks administrations — single-year data can misstate the trend in either direction.
